name: Armenian
image_alt: Long ribbed pale green cucumber coiled on a vine
description: A long, pale green, ribbed cucumber that is technically a muskmelon (Cucumis melo) but used entirely as a cucumber. Exceptionally heat and drought tolerant, never bitter, and produces through summer heat that defeats true cucumbers.
history: Originated in Armenia and spread through the Middle East and Mediterranean. Grown in America since colonial times. Thomas Jefferson cultivated it at Monticello. Known by many names — snake melon, yard-long cucumber, painted serpent.
seasonal_quirks: Thrives in conditions that stress true cucumbers — high heat, lower water. Being a melon, it needs heat to perform well. Grows extremely long (up to 3 feet) if left on the vine — harvest regularly.
pests_diseases: aphids — generally less pest pressure than true cucumbers
harvest_cues: Best at 12–15 inches — pale green, slightly ribbed, firm. Coils naturally on the vine. Left longer it turns yellow and develops a melon character. Cut rather than pull to avoid vine damage.
variety_notes: Never needs peeling — the thin skin is completely edible. Flesh is extremely mild with no bitterness even when slightly overripe. One of the best choices for hot, dry climates.
preservation: Quick pickle in sumac and salt. Refrigerator pickles.
